name: Elder
description: The Elder, or Elderberry (Sambucus nigra), holds a significant place in European folklore and tradition. Revered for its protective and healing properties, the Elder was often seen as a sacred tree, inhabited by the Elder Mother, also known as Hyldemoer in Scandinavian folklore. Respect for the tree was paramount; permission was often sought before cutting or using any part of it. The Elder was believed to ward off evil spirits, protect against witchcraft, and bring good fortune. Planting an Elder tree near a home was thought to safeguard the inhabitants from harm. Its branches were sometimes hung over doorways or windows for protection. Medicinally, the Elder has been used for centuries to treat colds, flu, and other ailments. The berries and flowers are rich in antioxidants and are believed to boost the immune system. The Elder's association with death and rebirth is also evident in some traditions. It was sometimes planted on graves, symbolizing the cyclical nature of life. The tree's connection to the underworld and the fairy realm further enhanced its mystical reputation. In various rituals, Elder wood was used to create wands and other magical tools. The Elder's versatility, both practically and symbolically, cemented its position as a cherished and powerful plant in European culture. The Elder Mother was considered a powerful being, capable of both blessing and cursing those who interacted with her tree. Proper respect and offerings were often given to appease her and ensure a positive outcome. Even today, the Elder continues to be appreciated for its beauty, medicinal properties, and rich cultural heritage.
subtitle: Sacred protector, healer, and guardian.

keyMyths.title: The Elder Mother
keyMyths.description: Hyldemoer, or the Elder Mother, is believed to reside within the Elder tree and protects it. Disrespecting the tree could incur her wrath, while treating it with reverence could bring blessings and good fortune.

keyMyths.title: Elder and Witchcraft
keyMyths.description: Elder was thought to be both a protection against and a tool for witchcraft. Some believed that witches could transform into Elder trees, while others used Elder wood in their magical practices.

sources.author: Jacob Grimm
sources.description: Explores the role of the Elder tree in Germanic folklore and mythology.
sources.text: Explores the role of the Elder tree in Germanic folklore and mythology.

sources.author: Margaret Baker
sources.description: Details the folklore and traditions associated with various plants, including the Elder.
sources.text: Details the folklore and traditions associated with various plants, including the Elder.

extendedContent.title: The Elder Mother and Sacred Respect
extendedContent.content: Central to the Elder's mythology is the Elder Mother, a spirit or goddess believed to reside within the tree. Known as Hyldemoer in Scandinavian regions, she was considered the tree's guardian and a powerful entity. As such, great respect was shown to the Elder. People would often ask permission before cutting branches or using the wood, sometimes offering gifts or apologies to appease the Elder Mother. Disrespecting the tree was thought to bring misfortune or illness upon the offender. This reverence extended to the entire plant, with various rituals and customs surrounding its harvesting and use. The Elder Mother's presence reinforced the tree's status as a sacred and potent symbol within European folklore, connecting the natural world with the spiritual realm.

extendedContent.title: Protective and Healing Properties
extendedContent.content: The Elder was widely regarded for its protective qualities. Planting an Elder tree near a home was believed to ward off evil spirits, witches, and other malevolent forces. Sprigs of Elder were often hung above doorways or carried as amulets for personal protection. In addition to its protective abilities, the Elder was also valued for its medicinal properties. Elderflower and elderberries were used to treat a variety of ailments, including colds, flu, and skin conditions. The flowers were often infused in teas or made into syrups, while the berries were used in jams, wines, and medicinal preparations. The combination of protective and healing attributes solidified the Elder's reputation as a powerful and benevolent plant in European folk medicine and magic.

extendedContent.title: Elder in Ritual and Divination
extendedContent.content: Beyond its practical uses, the Elder played a role in various rituals and divination practices. Some traditions involved using Elder branches to predict the weather, with the direction of the branches indicating the direction from which the wind would blow. The tree was also associated with death and rebirth, and in some regions, it was planted on graves to protect the deceased. Furthermore, the Elder's wood was sometimes used to create musical instruments, particularly pipes and flutes, believed to possess magical qualities. The association with music further enhanced the Elder's mystical aura, linking it to creativity, expression, and the unseen world. These ritualistic and divinatory uses underscore the Elder's deep integration into the cultural and spiritual fabric of European societies.

habitat: The Elder thrives in sunny locations with moist, well-drained soil, commonly found in hedgerows, woodlands, and along roadsides throughout Europe.
shortDescription: A sacred tree in European folklore, believed to be inhabited by the Elder Mother. Revered for protection and healing.
